coredata.dc:title: Recent progress of nanozymes with different spatial dimensions for bioanalysis
coredata.dc:creator: An, Mengying
He, Meng-Qi
Lin, Caishi
Wu, Yuebo
Ai, Yongjian
Xin, Hongbo
Liang, Qionglin
coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes are nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme-like properties. Since the landmark research report on nanozymes in 2007, numerous scientists focus on the topic of nanozyme. As an artificial enzyme, nanozymes take advantage of good stability, easy modification, designability, ease of preparation, and low cost. In recent years, due to the explosion of nanotechnology, biomimetic science, catalytic science, and theoretical computing, a great number of nanozymes have been fabricated. To highlight these achievements and allow the related researchers to grasp the current research status of structure-dependent nanozymes, the state-of-the-art in nanozymes from different spatial dimensions (0D, 1D, 2D, 3D) to the bioanalysis applications were reviewed. For different spatial dimensions nanozymes, the 0D nanodot, nanosphere, nanocluster; 1D nanorod, nanotube, 2D nanosheet, lamellar structure, and 3D metal-organic frameworks, covalent organic frameworks, hierarchical structure, and hydrogel were all discussed. Furthermore, the nanozymes applied for disease diagnosis, tumor microenvironment sensing, pathogen detection, drug detection, food detection, and environmental sensing were all discussed. Finally, the current challenges faced in nanozymology are outlined and the future directions for advancing nanozyme research are outlooked. We hope this review will inspire related research on nanozymes and contribute to developing nanozymes in bioanalysis.

Artificial enzymes
Structure-dependent activities
Biosensing
Bioanalysis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes are nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme-like properties. Since the landmark research report on nanozymes in 2007, numerous scientists focus on the topic of nanozyme. As an artificial enzyme, nanozymes take advantage of good stability, easy modification, designability, ease of preparation, and low cost. In recent years, due to the explosion of nanotechnology, biomimetic science, catalytic science, and theoretical computing, a great number of nanozymes have been fabricated. To highlight these achievements and allow the related researchers to grasp the current research status of structure-dependent nanozymes, the state-of-the-art in nanozymes from different spatial dimensions (0D, 1D, 2D, 3D) to the bioanalysis applications were reviewed. For different spatial dimensions nanozymes, the 0D nanodot, nanosphere, nanocluster; 1D nanorod, nanotube, 2D nanosheet, lamellar structure, and 3D metal-organic frameworks, covalent organic frameworks, hierarchical structure, and hydrogel were all discussed. Furthermore, the nanozymes applied for disease diagnosis, tumor microenvironment sensing, pathogen detection, drug detection, food detection, and environmental sensing were all discussed. Finally, the current challenges faced in nanozymology are outlined and the future directions for advancing nanozyme research are outlooked. We hope this review will inspire related research on nanozymes and contribute to developing nanozymes in bioanalysis.

In order to highlight these achievements and allow researchers to grasp the current research status of structure-dependent nanozymes, we reviewed the nanozymes from 0D, 1D, 2D, 3D spatial dimensions to their applications of bioanalysis. Finally, the current challenges faced in nanozymology are outlined and the future directions for advancing nanozyme research are outlooked.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the basis of various catalytic reactions, enzymes can make the ordinary reaction possess thousands of times the catalytic rate [1]. However, with the wide application of enzymes in the early 1900s, many unstable factors and shortcomings during reactions have been exposed. Artificial enzymes have attracted researchers’ attention with the development of biomimetic and organic chemistry. Through the combination of substrates and catalytically active functional groups, small molecules such as cyclodextrin [2], calixarene receptor binding [3] were used to prepare artificial enzymes. Subsequently, artificial enzymes based on amino acids or peptides as characteristic molecules expanded the field of analog enzymes, such as hemoglobin [4], and tyrosinase [5]. The emergence of artificial enzymes gradually makes up for the deficiencies of natural enzymes. Nanozymes were studied since the 1990s, Scrimin et al. put forward the term ‘nanozyme’ in a study that used a thiol monolayer to modify gold nanoparticles (NPs) to mimic ribonucleases in 2004 [6]. Since Yan et al. first discovered that Fe3O4 NPs have intrinsic peroxidase (POD)-like activity, which made the NPs systematically used as enzyme mimics for the first time [7]. Over the past 15 years, more and more studies demonstrated that metal atoms, metal oxides, and carbon compounds have POD, catalase (CAT), oxidase (OXD), and superoxide dismutase-like (SOD) enzyme activities [8]. In addition, previous literature has proved that nanozymes have the advantages of high stability, low cost, simple preparation, and easy modification [9–11] To date, it is reported that more than 30 countries have established over 600 laboratories in studies of nanozyme worldwide.

Although the specific definition of nanozymes is still debated, it is undeniable that the catalytic activity of nanozymes is closely related to the physical and chemical properties of the material itself [12] such as the nanomaterials’ size, shape, composition, and surface properties [13]. Many studies showed that the smaller size of the nanozyme leads to excellent catalytic activity [14]. Size-dependent behaviors can be explained by the reasons including but not limited to the higher specific surface area of the nanozymes, the electronic states on the surface of nanomaterials are different from those on the inside counterpart. Nanomaterials with different morphologies have remarkable properties in some aspects and the catalytic reaction mainly occurs on the surface of nanomaterials. As a kind of special nanomaterials, nanozymes with different morphology and structure, their specific surface area, exposure surface, and the number of active sites that participate in the reaction are different, thus resulting in nanozyme with unique activity [15,16].

A great number of literature show that nanozymes with the morphology of zero-dimension (0D), one-dimension (1D), two-dimension (2D), or three-dimension (3D) complex structures and the intrinsic properties of nanozymes are depended on the nanostructure [17]. In order to help researchers to understand the influence of nanostructure on catalytic activities, we summarized the recent progress of structure-dependent nanozymes. Furthermore, we also comment nanozymes are applied for biosensing and bioanalysis (Fig. 1 ). The authors hope that this review will be helpful and enlightening to researchers in the field of nanozyme, nanomaterials, and bioanalysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different dimensional structure and activity of nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials can be divided into four categories according to their spatial dimension, namely 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D. 0D nanoscale materials refer to substances in the three-dimensional dimension of space, such as NPs and quantum dots (QDs). Among them, the earliest and most widely studied NPs prepared from various metals and metal oxides show different enzyme activities depending on the intrinsic properties of metals. Since carbon nanotubes were first reported in 1991 [18], 1D nanomaterials have entered the field of vision. These materials typically grow along a non-nanoscale direction, ranging in length from nanometer to micron, such as nanowires [19] and nanorods (NRs), while remaining nanoscale in diameter. However, in practical application, people gradually found that the simple 0D nanomaterials have poor stability and tend to self-agglomeration in the reaction process, which leads to the inactivation of part of the active site, affecting the catalytic activity of the enzyme. In order to make up for the defects of the above materials, 2D nanomaterials represented by graphene have been gradually developed [20]. It is a kind of material with infinite plane size and molecular thickness, and its special structure brings many new properties [21]. In addition, as a carrier of 0D NPs, the stability of the overall composite material has been greatly improved. Beyond the nanoscale, 3D nanomaterials have more excellent properties, such as rich pore structure, high specific surface area, and low dielectric constant [22]. The above characteristics can effectively solve the problem of easy accumulation of low-dimensional nano-materials, retain the active sites of each component to the maximum extent, improve the stability and activity of the enzyme, and become the current research direction of nanozyme [23]. In this part, we will summarize the recent progress of nanozymes with the spatial dimensions of 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D. Furthermore, the structure-dependent enzymes-like activities were also discussed (Fig. 2 ).

0D nanomaterials do not exceed 100 nm in size in any dimension, so they show unique quantum size effects, including NPs [24], QDs [25], nanoclusters (NCs) [26], and nanospheres [27], in addition to the ultra-small size also provides some unique optical properties and enzyme activity for nanozymes.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of carbon-based nanomaterial, has good biocompatibility and unique optical properties. In recent years, it has aroused researchers’ wide interest [28,29]. For example, Shivalkar et al. prepared CDs in an environmentally friendly way by baking and crushing potato skins with high starch content, adjusting pH to alkaline, and then heating them in the microwave to get a black product. In addition, the experimental group added ferric oxide NP precursors with different content ratios to CDs to regulate CAT-like activity and magnetism, resulting in “microrobots” for pollution treatment [30]. Generally speaking, the smaller the size of the catalyst, the larger the surface area and the more conducive to the reaction. When the size of nanomaterials is controlled within a certain range, they have special effects [31,32]. In order to obtain CDs with longer absorption regions, Zhang et al. irradiated them with a 365 nm light-emitting diode. Surprisingly, the CDs after light treatment (light-treated C-dots) not only expanded the absorption of visible light but also significantly increased their photocatalytic efficiency to 12 times the original. The characterization results showed that the average particle diameter of the light-treated C-dots increased from 1.89 nm to about 2.99 nm. Further experiments speculated that it might be the occurrence of self-assembly induced by photocatalytic free radicals, and the activities of OXD and POD of the light-treated C-dots in dark were greatly enhanced [33]. Cu5.4O with an average size of only about 4 nm has amazing activity and metabolic advantages [34], and although more complex materials have higher stability, faster metabolism and lower toxicity are preferred in in vivo applications of nanozymes.

In addition to size, other factors such as structural defects can also influence the active site. Studies have shown that compared with other nano-morphologies and metal types, molybdenum oxide 0D nanodots (NDs) have smaller sizes and richer surface defects, which can expose more active sites. Xu et al. constructed oxygen-vacancy molybdenum trioxide NDs (MoO3-x NDs) with an average diameter of about 3 nm via the one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The NDs exhibited good POD-like activity, and more interestingly, under the irradiation of near-infrared (NIR), the temperature gradually increased to the optimum temperature for the activity of the nanozyme. The activity of POD was further increased [35]. Yang et al. prepared Au–Bi2Se3 NDs by reduction method (Fig. 3 (A)). The electron donation effect of Bi2Se3 NDs and abundant Bi defects in lattice structure provided obvious support for electron transfer, greatly improving the catalytic activity of the multi-enzyme analog, including the POD, SOD, CAT, and GPx-like activities [36].

For catalytic enzymes, affinity with substrates is also an important factor affecting catalytic capacity. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozyme lacks substrate binding and high specificity. So to solve the problem of combining with the target molecular targets, Willner et al. used the adapter body of high selectivity and affinity, with the Cu2+CD covalent coupling, the modified version of the adapter body, enzyme specificity, catalytic reactions, and the adapter body unit can improve response near the substrate concentration, significantly improving the catalytic efficiency (Fig. 3(B)) [37]. In addition to adaptation, some special antibodies and peptides can also make the nanozyme have specific targeting properties. To integrate tumor therapy and imaging diagnosis, Wang et al. prepared core-shell structure nanozyme using seed-mediated growth of Ag2S QDs and Fe2C QDs and connected T Helper1, the therapeutic target of breast cancer, to the outermost end of the system to play a targeted role (Fig. 3(C-D)) [38]. Because of the special optical properties of CDs, researchers often control their activity utilizing external light stimulation [39]. Therefore, Sun et al. proposed a novel fluorescent nanozyme, which doped Mn (ⅱ) into Ag2Se QDs through cationic doping, and the resulting nanozyme had good emission imaging in the NIR-Ⅱ. Further modified with arginine–glycine–aspartate tripeptide and polyethylene glycol, the nanoprobes obtained have a good tumor-targeting effect and will eventually be used in tumor imaging and radiotherapy [25]. Luo et al. prepared Fe-doped CDs (Fe@CDs) using ferric phthalocyanine and modified them with amphiphilic lipopeptide components to enhance tumor targeting. With a high photothermal conversion efficiency of 63.4% and POD-like activity, the whole system can integrate photothermal, photodynamic, and imaging diagnosis (Fig. 3(E)) [40].

Nanospheres are the simplest kind of particle, and their radius is the only tunable parameter compared to other materials. Therefore, its enzyme activity is mainly determined by components. For example, Wang et al. prepared iron phosphate nanospheres (FePOs) by hydrothermal method. Surprisingly, nanocrystalline iron phosphate showed a variety of enzyme activities. Under acidic conditions, FePOs showed good POD-like activity, while under neutral conditions, they showed similar SOD and CAT activities [41]. In addition to a single component, synergistic strategies of different materials are often used to enhance enzyme activity. Mao et al. prepared Pt nano-alloy by reduction–oxidation (redox) reaction, and the characterization results showed that the PtCu NAs were uniformly dispersed in a spherical shape. Based on the properties of the two metals, the nanozyme has a strong antioxidant capacity, integrating the activities of POD, CAT, and SOD. In addition, the ratio of the two metal contents also affects the activity of the enzyme. The particle size and crystal structure of the nano-alloy are adjusted by changing the mole ratio of Pt2+/Cu2+. With the increase of the platinum ratio, the diameter of the PtCu NAs decreases and the activity of the nanozyme increases significantly [42]. In the study published by Zhang, Fe3O4@Pt@E5 modified by E5 was prepared by high-temperature pyrolysis and deposition, so this nanozyme has the targeting ability of acute myeloid leukemia. As expected, the spherical Fe3O4@Pt@E5 showed excellent POD, CAT, SOD, and unexpected OXD-like activity, indicating a synergistic enhancement effect of the two materials used in combination [43].

In terms of using synthase activity, more researchers have found that using the catalytic activity of biomolecules to synthesize nanomaterials can significantly improve their biocompatibility [44]. Xu et al. combined the hemin molecule with polypyrrole (PPy) to create nanospheres about 60 nm in size. PPy forms a protective coating on the surface of self-assembled heme NPs by in situ polymerization. We then verified that HNPs@PPy had a significant POD-like activity compared with heme NPs (HNPs) using 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein, presumably because the affinity between the enzyme and H2O2 was improved by hydrogen bonding, and the stability of the nanozyme was enhanced by the PPy coating [46]. At the same time, the research group also mixed different metal particles into ferritin nanocages (FTn) and prepared a variety of metal nanozymes including FTn-Pt, FTn-Rh, FTn-Au, FTn-Ir, and so on. Hydroxyphenyl fluorescein was used to determine the enzyme activity, and the results showed that FTn-Pt had the highest POD-like activity [47]. Zhang et al. designed an artificial hybridization enzyme to combine Mn-based NPs with human protein-derived nanocells derived from gene recombination. The results showed that MnO2 fenozyme was a monodisperse spherical structure with an average diameter of about 6.5 nm, and the MnO2 nanocrystalline was enclosed in it. Under the same reaction conditions, the enzyme activities of Fe3O4 fenozyme were compared. The results showed that the CAT activity of MnO2 fenozyme was similar to that of Fe3O4 fenozyme but much higher than that of SOD, and the POD activity was very low [48]. The enzyme activity often needs to be further designed and regulated according to the specific physiological environment in the body. For example, Zhang et al. developed a pH-sensitive graphene nanozyme to treat Helicobacter pylori, a bacterium that causes a range of stomach ulcers, in combination with the acidic environment of the stomach, using a one-pot method to wrap PtCo in a graphene shell to form PtCo@G. Using the outermost layer of graphene in harsh conditions to protect the characteristics of the internal metal undisturbed, when nanozyme is into the stomach, the class OXD activity is activated, the local accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) concentration in order to play the effect of sterilization, but after entering the intestinal, neutral condition makes the enzyme activity is inhibited and less toxic to other microorganisms [49].

In addition to spherical NPs, hollow nanospheres also have great potential. The hollow shell structure not only has a huge specific surface area but also provides a good platform for material delivery and reaction. Lin et al. prepared spherical particles with hollow mesoporous structure and then deposited Pt on the outer layer by vacuum metal sputtering deposition technology. Combined with vacancy defects, the final particle size of Pt–TiO2 was about 100 nm [50]. Importantly, this nanostructure not only promotes electron transfer but also has CAT-like activity, which improves the internal hypoxia environment after entering the tumor cell [51]. It is worth mentioning that the doping of some elements can significantly accelerate the catalytic reaction rate. Liu et al. added iron phthalocyanine (FePc) to N-doped carbon nanospheres that were pyrolytically etched to produce hollow structures, resulting in FePc/HNCS. Based on the presence of π electron coupling pairs, the nanozyme had good POD and CAT activities [52]. Furthermore, Fan et al. used density functional theory (DFT) to predict the boron (B), phosphorus (P), and sulfur (S), after several non-metallic elements-doped carbon materials of enzyme activity, and finally designed a kind of phosphorus and N double-doped porous hollow carbon spheres (PNCNzymes), as expected, the enzyme show superior POD-like activity, and within a certain range, there was a linear correlation between enzyme activity and phosphorus content (Fig. 3(F–G)). It may be related to the increase of active sites and the synergistic enhancement of enzyme activity by phosphorus doping [45].

NCs refer to molecule-level aggregates composed of several to dozens of atoms, while metal NCs are nanomaterials with metal cores modified by different ligands on the surface [53]. Kuang et al. prepared CuxO NCs using Cu ion as the precursor and L-phenylalanine (L-Phe) as the structural guiding agent. Meanwhile, the ligand was replaced with several other amino acids as the control group, and the activity of these NCs was compared. The results showed that among the four enzymes with different morphology, CuxO-Phe showed the highest SOD-like and CAT-like enzyme activities, which was related to its largest specific surface area and special ligand [54]. Studies have shown that, despite the overall topology, enzyme on the surface of the microenvironment of the combination of the substrate and the active site and enzyme activity has a great influence, in combination with NCs' different effects on the ligand [55] Liu et al. choose arginine to provide close to the natural microenvironment of enzymes, through the reduction reaction design of the shape of a tree blackberry peptide-Pt hybrid nanozyme. This enzyme has excellent uricase-like activity. Compared with natural uricase, Protamine-PtNCs not only have similar enzyme activity sites but also have similar substrate binding ability. In addition, the presence of arginine residues also increases enzyme activity, which also provides some data for the future application of nanozyme in the treatment of gout [26]. This pathway for designing nanozymes mediated by natural structures may provide new ideas for the development of other inorganic nanomaterials.

The sizes of nanomaterials are controlled at 1–100 nm, providing a high aspect ratio, so many similar morphologies are classified as 1D nanomaterials. Given the homogeneous structure, superior electron conduction ducts, and sufficient exposed surface sites, nanozymes based on this structure offer many opportunities for functional modification.

Compared to other 1D structures, solid NRs can be synthesized from a wide variety of materials, and the synthesis methods are flexible. Several common precious metals and metal oxides are studied with high research rates. Liu et al. prepared porous cerium oxide NRs (PN–CeO2 NRs) by hydrothermal method and further modified them with sodium polyacrylamide sulfonate (PN-CeO2-PSS) to better capture the reaction substrate and oxygen. And the catalytic activity of oxygen was improved by OXD [56]. Due to the good chemical stability and can provide tunable size and shape of Au, there are more kinds of nanozymes related to Au in recent years. Jing et al. found that 1D Au NRs have absorbability of light from visible to NIR [13]. To reduce material consumption and cost, many researchers combine different metals, Engelbrekt et al. combining Au with Pt offer the benefit of improved bimetallic properties and diminished levels of Pt loading. It has been reported that anisotropic metal heterostructures can be obtained by transverse etching of core-shell NRs or surface coatings [57]. Therefore, Lu et al. synthesized anisotropic dumbbell-like Au–Pt nanostructures using DNA programming strategies, taking advantage of the strong interaction between DNA and the {110} and {100} surfaces of Au NRs [58], the fact that DNA is more closely aligned on both sides than on the ends and it can control the shape of the metal system in a sequentially dependent manner. The obtained Au–Pt nanozyme has high photothermal conversion efficiency and excellent POD-like activity under NIR-Ⅱ radiation [59]. Interestingly, for the same dumbbell-like structure, He et al. bonded CeO2 NPs at both ends of Au NRs by hydrothermal synthesis. Plasmonic nanostructures are considered to have good catalytic prospects due to their superior light capture and conversion capabilities. In this study, a series of instruments were used to study the mechanism of surface plasmon resonance's (SPR's) activation of Au@CeO2 POD activity in the field of sterilization [60].

In addition to the strategy of using the different types of metal superposition [61,62], researchers often adjust the surface of the nanozyme to improve the enzyme activity, such as surface chemistry [63]. Wu et al. found that when the surface of Au NRs was modified with L-cysteine, the self-POD activity was significantly increased. Further investigation showed that the carboxyl group of the grafted cysteine would have a strong electrostatic interaction with the amino group of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), thus greatly enhancing the affinity of the substrate. In addition, they used glutathione (GSH) as a control and found that the activity of Au NRs modified by GSH was not significantly improved because the size of GSH was larger than that of L-cysteine, resulting in a larger spatial distance between the modified nanozyme and TMB and a lower affinity between them [64]. Some scholars have found that electron distribution and transfer around the active site can directly affect the activity of the enzyme when discussing the structure–activity relationship of nanozymes [65,66]. Some enzymes' activities are related to the electron pairs in the NRs. Namrata et al. synthesized cerium vanadate NRs (CeVO4 NRs) by hydrothermal method. Based on previous reports that CeO2 relies on the ratio of Ce3+ to Ce4+ to simulate enzyme activity [67], they verified the good SOD-like activity of nanozyme using colorimetry. Interestingly, they found that the NRs had no OXD and POD-like activities, although both Ce and V particles had these enzyme activities [68]. Cao et al. modified the enzyme using Bi2MoO6 (BMO), and then the Ag particles were reduced to them. Finally, the rod-like Fe3O4/Ag/BMO NPs were obtained by thermal doping. The synthase has POD-like, CAT-like, and SOD-like activities. Then, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and other methods were used to analyze the enzymatic catalytic reaction process, and it was found that the activity may be derived from the electron cycle of Fe2+/Fe3+ and Mo5+/Mo6+ redox couple (Fig. 4 (A)) [69].

Hydrogen-bonded organic frameworks (HOFs) are a crystalline porous skeleton material formed by hydrogen bonding of organic or metal-organic building units. In recent years, it has been studied more and more because of its large specific surface area, mild synthetic conditions, and adjustable pore structure [70]. Ye et al. obtained HBF-1-C800 NRs by limiting bovine serum protein in N-doped porous carbon framework through high-temperature pyrolysis. Thanks to the increased location defects of the porous structure and the increase of the active site after N doping, the POD activity of the enzyme is 3–7 times higher than that of a traditional carbon-based nanozyme [71]. Compared with the preparation of NRs by template framework, we synthesized NRs by metal–ligand crosslinking strategy through self-assembly between Fe3+ and dopamine. After calcining and carbonization, iron was dispersed in particles of about 3 nm between nanoporous rods. Enzyme test results showed that the NRs had superb OXD and POD-like enzyme activities, which were closely related to the ultra-small active center and good atomic utilization (Fig. 4(B)) [72].

Due to the large specific surface area of nanotubes, the increase in active sites by reason of incomplete coordination of surface atoms, and the unique luminal structure, they are becoming increasingly popular in the design of cavity nanoreactors. Lu et al. successfully generated N-doped carbon tubes that limit Fe NPs in their interior by pyrolysis, and due to the sharp increase in the concentration of active sites in the lumen, coupled with a closed structure that can accelerate electron transport, the POD-like activity of Fe@NC nanotubes is superior to that of Fe NPs or carbon tubes alone [73]. Based on the limited effect and synergy, in other published studies, Lu et al. prepared PPy nanotubes containing NiO and CoO NPs in the inner cavity by calcination and thermal reduction, and the concentration of the reaction center in the tube made the nanozyme have amazing POD-like and CAT-like activities [74]. In recent years, Prussian blue (PB) has been found to contain a large number of adjustable transition metal ions with a variety of enzyme activities [75]. Therefore, He et al. used PB coral-like cobalt cyanide (CoHCF) as a precursor and Co instead of Fe to prepare bamboo-like N-doped carbon nanotube encapsulated Co (N-CNTs@Co) nanomaterial through high-temperature pyrolysis. Characterization results showed that the carbon nanomaterial was linear and helical, and some Co particles were encapsulated in the activity center of the simulated enzyme in the nanotube. N-CNTs@Co has excellent OXD-like activity, which is 12 times higher than most of the reported CeO2 activity. In addition, changing the ratio of K3[Co(CN)6] to Co(NO3)2 can obtain nanotubes of different lengths, and when the ratio of the two is 1.0:1.5, the enzyme activity is the highest. This may be related to the accumulation and leakage of Co NPs inside the tube [76].

Of course, in addition to being a carrier, nanotubes can also play a good catalytic role in chemical reactions with their unique structure. Lu et al. used electrospinning, deposition, calcination, and other methods to produce CuO/NiO nanotubes, and the rough flake-like structure of the surface increased the contact area during the reaction, while the rapid increase in nanotube-like POD activity was due to the effective electron transport rate and special tubular structure between CuO and NiO [77]. Therefore, for the special structure of nanotubes, the composition of its components and the proportion between them will have a great impact on the catalytic efficiency. However, it is necessary to pay attention to the preparation cost and operability.

Researchers often define sheet nanomaterials with a certain transverse size to thickness ratio as 2D nanomaterials, and their very high specific surface area, unique surface chemical functions, and other extraordinary physical and chemical properties have attracted many researchers to develop sheet nanozymes, such as black phosphorus (BP) nanosheets [78], boron (B)-containing nanosheets, graphene and 2D metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [79,80]. In addition, exposure to active sites also provides excellent opportunities for surface functionalization. Therefore, in the preparation of highly active nanozymes, the sheet structure is a good choice.

When atoms are confined to the plane, the specific surface area of the material is greatly increased while preserving the thickness of the atom. In addition, the high exposure of surface sites is also convenient for researchers to carry out surface modification and other means, so 2D materials show unique advantages. Based on this, Cheng's study prepared a bimetallic oxide FeWOX by organic phase thermal decomposition. Fe-based nanozymes generally have enzyme-like activity. TMB was also used to verify that this nanosheet has a good POD activity [81]. Li et al. prepared a black phosphorus–platinum (BP–Pt) nanocomposite through the situ reduction reaction of Pt2+ on the surface of BPNSs. This BP–Pt nanozyme showed a high affinity for H2O2 and POD-like activity [82]. Wu et al. synthesized the average transverse size is 150 nm, a slice thickness of 4.5 nm MnNS, making MnNS have a lager surface area and the largest surface active sites, promoting the increase of enzyme activity; therefore, the nanometer sheet has super OXD-like and POD-like activity. It can maximize the use of O2 as a co-reaction agent for the catalytic oxidation reactions [83]. Li et al. added Mo5+ and Co2+ into Se2− solution and constructed MoSe2/CoSe2 nanosheets by a co-deposition method. The redox cycle between Co3+/Mo6+ and Co2+/Mo4+ resulted in the POD-like and CAT-like activities of the nanozyme (Fig. 5 (A)). In addition, due to the reduction of resistance and the improvement of charge transfer, the nanozyme activity of the heterostructure was nearly three times that of the pure MoSe2 [84]. More interestingly, Gao et al. accidently found that SnSe nanosheets exhibit dehydrogenase-like activity to catalyze the dehydrogenation of organic molecules while evaluating the cycle activity of tricarboxylic acid in cells. Furthermore, the specificity and high efficiency of the nanosheet catalysis were verified by comparing the dehydrogenation of 25 different nanozymes. In addition, to assess the effect of the form of activity, the comparison of the different sizes of nanofilms and nanosphere, the results show that the SnSe of nanometer catalytic activity increased with the increase of the layer number reduced, the surface of the negatively charged single nanometer active highest, guess reaction may occur in between medium and atomic layer interface [85].

However, some studies have shown that metal or metal-oxide-based nanozymes are unstable and easily oxidized under acidic conditions and have potential biological toxicity in vivo [88]. Therefore, the development of metal-free nanozymes with good enzyme activity has become a hot topic. Graphene is often applied in the field of catalysis due to a series of excellent physical and chemical properties brought by its special atomic arrangement [89,90]. Mrinmoy De et al. selected 2, 4, 6-tribromo-3-hydroxybenzoic acid as raw material and FeCl2 as a coupling agent to synthesize 2D graphene nanosheets with good POD activity through the bottom-up method. Subsequently, modification strategies were applied to further improve the enzyme activity of graphene [91]. Given that in situ pyrolysis maximizes the preservation of the precursor chemical structure, Li et al. thermally converted the N-rich porous structure to QAU-Z1 with a graphene structure, which demonstrated superior OXD-like activity in the metal-free nanozyme range due to its rare N content (about 8%) [92]. Bi et al. prepared B-doped graphdiyne (B-GDY) nanosheets by cross-coupling with a new 2D organic material-GDY. Compared with GDY without B doping, B atoms replace carbon atoms as the reactive center due to the different electronegativity after the addition of boron, and the number of active sites is greatly increased, so B-GDY has excellent POD activity, which is used in nanozyme antibacterial therapy [93]. In addition, Sui et al. also synthesized palladium-iron graphene nanosheets (PdFe/GDY) by hydrothermal method with the advantage of GDY's high substrate affinity and good stability, and the obtained nanozyme had splendid POD-like enzyme activity due to the presence of Pd element (Fig. 5(B)). Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate oxidase (NOX) also plays a special role in the body's immunity and reactive oxygen generation [86]. According to the structure characteristics of NOX play a physiological role in the body, Li et al. by a thermal method of Fe–N-doped graphene prepared nanosheets, then through catalytic product ultraviolet and high-performance liquid chromatography analysis to determine the nanozyme has good NOX simulation activity and compared with the natural enzyme, the reaction condition is broader, more stable enzyme activity [94]. Gai et al. successfully synthesized the first metal-free carbon-based nanozyme with only NOX enzyme activity by high-temperature pyrolysis, which not only had an enzymatic activity of 3.2 U/mg but also lifted the confinement of pH < 7.5 to the catalytic effect of natural enzymes [95].

Most of the active sites of nanozyme are distributed on the surface of the frame and in the internal space, and the substrate molecules have the characteristics of a wide diffusion range and long diffusion distance, which can easily hinder its catalytic activity. However, the 2D MOF nanozyme has an ultra-thin thickness and large surface area, and the active site is more dispersed, which can effectively improve the accessibility of substrate molecules to the active site [96]. Hu et al. prepared MOFs with typical ultra-thin sheet nanostructure. At the same time, AuNPs were grown on MOFs by in-situ reduction of HAuCl4 using NaBH4 as a reducing agent. The results showed that AuNPs were evenly distributed on the surface of MOF. Due to the abundance of active sites exposed in 2D MOFs and the low transfer resistance between substrate molecules and active sites, the nanozyme has excellent POD-like activity [97]. Shi et al. wanted to expose more active sites to achieve the desired therapeutic effect, so they designed a 2D MOF nanosheet of manganese porphyrin group (ZMTP) by bottom-up solvent method, and coordination reaction, a nanozyme with a thickness of only 4 nm, which provides a more accessible activity center for exerting amazing SOD-like and CAT-like enzyme activities. In addition, this unique structure further accelerates the degradation of ZMTP nanozymes in vivo [98,99].

Defect engineering is often considered the most efficient design for improving the properties of 2D materials [100,101]. Based on the structural characteristics of natural enzymes, the space construction of active sites during enzyme reaction was simulated by the aggregation and vacancy of NPs. Yu et al. introduced Fe into MoOx through the metal-polyphenol strategy to obtain Fe-MoOx nanozyme with a shape similar to that of a rose, the addition of Fe makes the entire class electron transfer speed, defect sites in the process of enzyme catalysis, and the Fe-MoOx have a kind of CAT, POD, OXD, and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide hydrogen oxidase (NOX) enzyme activity, They showed synergistic activity (Fig. 5(C)) [87]. Molybdenum sulfide (MoS2) makes the substrate adhere to the metal atoms by producing a large number of defect sites so that the electrons produced by the metal are directly absorbed by the metal atoms by the substrate to generate free radicals [102,103]. MoS2 contains a lot of defects that determines the size of the catalytic activity of the principle of preparation with thermodynamic stability compared with other researchers on the surface of the base MoS2 and led to the decrease of the enzyme properties, Wang et al. through in vertical growth on graphene-doped N redox MoS2, cracking defects crystal structure on the surface of the base project, found that under the guidance of DFT, the MoS2 vertical array/N-doped reduction graphene oxide nanozyme expressed good POD-like activity [104]. The use of defect engineering has led to more and more types of high-efficiency nanozymes, but at the same time, the simplicity of the preparation method should be considered, and the metal-ligand crosslinking strategy is worth further exploration.

Compared with low-dimensional nanomaterials with simple structures, 3D nanostructures tend to fuse different nanoscales in a single structure. Because the material often uses the known 3D structure as a framework, some researchers have focused on the structure–activity relationship of nanozymes and designed a batch of ultra-active nanozymes by biomimetic means.

MOFs are porous, crystal supramolecular organic–inorganic coordination systems containing many active sites, which are promising nanomaterials. To further exploit the unique structure of MOFs, numerous modification methods have been developed, for example, adding chemical functionality onto the surfaces or into the interior spaces of MOFs, such as metal oxide and metal NPs [105,106]. In recent years, single-atom enzymes (SAEs) have attracted extensive research because of their definite geometric structure, high efficiency of atom utilization, and unique quantum size structure [8,107]. Wang et al. successfully constructed Cu single-atom sites/N-doped porous carbon (Cu SASs/NPC) by a pyrolysis–etching–adsorption–pyrolysis strategy. The characterization results showed that Cu existed in the form of atomically dispersed NPC, thanks to more active sites being exposed, Cu SASs/NPC has stronger catalytic activity and the ability to consume GSH and also can act as POD-like nanozymes, catalyzing the conversion of H2O2 into •OH compared with the NPC without Cu doping [108]. Meanwhile, magnetic MOF composite materials have attracted extensive attention because of their unique magnetic recovery. To achieve or even exceed the catalytic capacity of natural enzymes, Ji et al. designed SAE with a well-defined electron and geometric configuration, referring to HRP's active center and 3D structure. P was regarded as the precursor to locate the active center, and then Fe ion and monomers of poly-(cyclotriphospazene-co-4,4'-diaminodiphenylether were coated on zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF)-8 to obtain the final Fe/ZIF-8@PZM. It was found that the Fe/ZIF-8@PZM had amazing POD-like activity (316 U/mg), and its specific activity value was more than 30 times that of the commonly used Fe3O4 nanozyme. According to the characterization results and the DFT calculation, it is inferred that the significant increase in activity is related to the electron donation of P atoms near the Fe site and the well-designed 3D porous framework (Fig. 6 (A)) [109]. Ma[Mb(CN)6], which has a unique cubic skeleton, is also considered a branch when MOFs are used as support material because of their various advantages [110]. Wang et al. reported that Mn3[Co(CN)6]2-based SAE has excellent CAT-like activity. Ru and photosensitivities are introduced into it by metal-organic coordination and other non-covalent interactions and assembled into OxgeMCC-r SAE, achieving the dual purpose of in vivo tracking and tumor therapy [111].

Manganese (Mn), as a cofactor of many metal enzymes, is one of the essential elements in the human body [112]. To explore the activity and role of Mn-based SAE, Zhu et al. used hollow ZIF-8 nanocubes treated with tannic acid to obtain Mn/SAE captured by porous carbon through ion exchange and pyrolysis. In order to improve the biocompatibility of the nanozyme, the surface was further coated to obtain polyethylene glycol Mn/PSAE. Then, a colorimetric method was used to verify that the Mn nanozyme had a variety of enzyme activities such as OXD, POD, and CAT, excluding the influence of frame. Moreover, the enzyme activity was significantly enhanced in the acidic microenvironment of the tumor [115]. In addition to Mn, Pd-based SAE is also frequently used in studies. Lin et al. prepared the Pd@ZIF-8 complex by pyrolysis, and to improve the biocompatibility of the enzyme, they used hS-PEG for surface modification. Interestingly, 5, 5′-dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) was used to determine the enzyme activity. It was found that the nanozyme mimicked the activity of glutathione oxidase (GSHOx) and also had better POD-like activity than ordinary nanozymes [116].

In addition to SAE, MOF can also be used as the carrier of other NPs. Li et al. constructed large and directionally ordered pores in a single MOF crystal and then loaded a variety of different ultra-small alloys by current displacement. The results showed that Cu–Pd@MIL-101 loaded with 9.5 wt% had the best SOD-like and POD-like activities due to the presence of the alloy and the synergistic effect of Cu–Pd alloy [117]. Xu et al. synthesized Fe3O4@MIL-100(Fe) in situ within 30 min by a microwave-assisted method, by studying the magnetic property and the enzyme activity, it was indicated that the prepared Fe3O4@MIL-100(Fe) had excellent superparamagnetic and obvious POD-like and CAT-like activity. In addition, during the synthesis process, we found that changing the ratio of Fe3O4 to H3BTC yielded Fe3O4@MIL-100(Fe) with different enzyme activities, which may be because too thick a shell increases the distance of the central catalytic site from the target or increases the clogging of the hole, while the appropriate MOF shell thickness can enhance the adsorption of the target [118]. What is more, the internal space of MOF can be used for enzyme fixation, and the integration of multiple enzyme activities can be realized by clever use of outer frame modification while stabilizing the enzyme structure. Cu-HCF was synthesized by Wang et al. using the strong coordination between Cu ions and the N-terminal of the connecter. In addition to the POD-like activity reported by previous studies, Cu-containing nanozyme also showed good GSHOx-like activity [119]. In order to avoid different enzymes acting alone, the overall efficiency of the catalytic system is reduced. Wei et al. prepared an integrated nanozyme—GOx/hemin@ZIF-8 by co-assembly strategy, which controlled the distance between enzymes through internal space, so that the primary enzyme product quickly diffused to the secondary enzyme reaction region, and efficient catalytic activity and cascade reaction system were obtained [120]. Kuang et al. further explored and found that Cu-MOF not only has higher catalytic efficiency in the weak acid environment but also makes the preparation method simpler and safer. Therefore, by encapsulating GOx, the activity of the overall nanozyme is also significantly enhanced due to the proximity effect [121]. In the framework, some metal nodes that are different from the original are introduced in an appropriate amount to facilitate the synergistic improvement of enzyme activity. Kuang et al. added Ni to MIL-53 to adjust the crystal structure, and the enzyme activity results showed that Ni only served as a substrate adsorption site, accelerating the substrate transport speed in the sub-nanometer channel of MIL-53(Fe) and increasing the catalytic rate [122]. Similarly, Wang's research also verified the synergistic enhancement of bimetals on enzyme activity and synthesized Mn/Fe-MIL(53) by one-pot method, and the reaction cycle of Fe2+/Fe3+ and Mn2+/Mn3+ coupling gave nanozymes outstanding OXD-like activity [123]. Zhang et al. designed a multilayer core-shell structure nanozyme. First, Fe3O4 was encapsulated by ZIF-8, and then GOx entered the pores of ZIF-8 by electrostatic absorption method. Finally, MnO2 was used as the outer thin layer to obtain Fe3O4@ZIF-8/GOx@MnO2 hybrid nanozyme. It is not surprising that the enzyme combines multiple enzyme activities to produce a good antitumor effect [124].

Due to the specificity of the structure, the heterogeneity of the components, and the fuzziness of the active site, researchers have been hindered from understanding the quantitative structure–activity relationship of artificial synthases and the further design high-performance nanozymes. The development of protein engineering reveals the relationship between the structure of natural enzymes and catalytic efficiency [125]. Inspired by this, Wu et al. used MIL-53(Fe) as an example to study the relationship between the structure of MOF nanozymes and their OXD-like activity. First, they demonstrated that the enzyme's activity originates from a MOF and, similar to that of natural OXD, has active sites on metal nodes. Then the substitution ligand was changed and it was found that the enzyme activity had different changes, especially the introduction of the nitro group significantly enhanced the enzyme activity. It was speculated that the electron-absorbing ligand made the metal node more devoid of electrons, thus promoting the electron transfer between substances. In addition, other OXD substrates and different types of MOFs were investigated to further verify the above results: the electronic effect of ligands is independent and universal, and there is a linear relationship with Hammett σm value (Fig. 6(B)) [113]. Based on these results, the group went on to investigate the relationship between ligand and activity in MIL-47(V)-X MOFs and found that the synthesized substance had the strongest GSH POD-like (GPx) activity after the introduction of amino groups (Fig. 6(C)) [114].

The synthesis of nanozymes with high stability and superb activity has always been the ideal goal pursued by researchers. In recent years, the transition metal–nitrogen-doped carbon (M–N–C) nanostructures have been considered the most promising catalysts due to their sufficient activity and stability [126,127]. Using ZIF-8 as a precursor, Chen et al. obtained Fe–N-doped porous carbon nanozyme (Fe–N–C) by introducing hemin. Therewith, by adjusting the hemin content to 55 mg, Fe–N sites were enriched and highly dispersed to obtain the nanozyme with the best POD-like activity [128]. According to experiments and DFT calculations, Liu et al. found that M–N–C with different metal types had different substrate selectivity. For example, Fe–N–C and Co–N–C show high but opposite selectivity when catalyzed by H2O2 due to the intrinsic difference of the intermediate M = O produced by the reaction. In addition, due to the ligand embedded in the M–N–C graphite framework, its selectivity is higher than the natural HRP [129].

Covalent organic framework (COF) [130] is a kind of organic polymer connected by covalent bond, which is composed of mesh chemistry with distinct composition and structure controllability. COFs were used as the framework of the nanozyme to regulate the enzyme activity by regulating the interaction between surface functional groups and the active site of the enzyme [131,132]. Therefore, Zhang et al. constructed a novel nanozyme (NMCTP-TTA) using NH-MIL-88B as the main model and phenol and triazine groups as ligands. Enzyme activity measurement showed that the POD activity of NMCTP-TTA was nearly 9 times higher than that of the main body before modification, which was related to the nature of amino transfer electron, and also corresponded with the pseudo burr structure significantly increased the surface area under an electron microscope [133]. In addition, ionic COFs are often used as an ideal platform for simulating natural enzymes. Studies showed that the catalytic activity of COFs-based nanozyme was significantly enhanced after chelating with a porphyrin ring [134]. Xie et al. reported that the Fe-chelated porphyrin-group ionic COFs was obtained through a solution reaction, and the final product GFeF nanozyme was obtained by adding GOX and centrifugation. The nanozyme can be used to simulate the activity of POD, SOD, and CAT [135].

Previous studies have shown that the introduction of other metals into metal NPs to construct polymetallic nanostructures (alloys and core-shell structures) contributes to increased enzyme activity, which is related to their composition, morphology, and size [136]. Au NPs have been widely used in nanomaterials due to their unique surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity and some catalytic properties similar to POD, CAT, and SOD [137]. Similarly, Pt NPs (less than 5 nm in diameter) also exhibit a variety of enzyme-like activities [50]. Fu et al. published an article about the preparation of Au NPs seeds and the formation of porous core-shell nanostructures by in-situ reduction of Pt4+ ions to Pt on the surface of Au NPs. Interestingly, the as-obtained Au@Pt NPs showed remarkably enhanced POD catalysis [138]. Using the same reduction reaction, Yang et al. designed a hollow CoO@AuPt nanozyme. Specifically, Co reduces PtCl6 2− and AuCl4− to Pt and Au atoms, and the remaining small amount of Co is oxidized into CoO by O2 in the air, eventually forming a thin AuPt shell around CoO. The CoO@AuPt NPs have both CAT-like and POD-like activity for H2O2, producing O2 and multiple ROS. In addition, it can also be used as an OXD of GSH. The reason for this phenomenon, in addition to the inherent of the metal, may also be related to the large surface exposure of the metal surface to provide more active high-index facets [139].

Similarly, in order to obtain artificial enzymes with various activities that can simulate the multi-enzyme system in the cell to protect the cell against oxidative stress, it is often a good strategy to prepare composite structures with various materials [140]. Thus, we have fabricated the multilayer Se@Me@MnO2 spheres with an average size of 150 nm. The Se@Me@MnO2 was fabricated by polymerization of dopamine and in situ redox reaction. The results showed that the nanozyme had POD, CAT, and SOD-like activities. Further exploring the specific mechanism, we found that the Se core was GPx-like enzyme mimics, the middle layer Me was CAT-like enzyme mimics, and the outermost layer MnO2 was SOD-like mimics [141]. Lin designed the composite by growing MoS2 nano-flowers on titanium dioxide nanotubes, which further enhanced the overall POD-like activity by virtue of the holes and good electron conduction of the nanotubes [142]. The size of the nanometer enzyme activity and its corresponding natural enzyme's active site exposure quantity and structure similarity should be positively correlated in theory [143]. Some researchers' eyes on the enzyme protein regulation engineering, for metal protease structure clear, by changing the metal sites around on active regulation and control the distribution of electronic. For example, Xu et al. found that the strong electron-absorbing substituent (-NO2) can optimize the electron distribution near the central site and adjust the shape of the adsorption intermediates, thus significantly enhancing the enzyme activity of MIL-101 [65].

Hydrogel, as a system with a 3D space network structure, has soft properties and strong water absorption capacity [144,145]. In addition, its biocompatibility and biodegradability make it more widely used in the biomedical field in recent years [146–148]. In terms of drug transport, hydrogels have the functions of storing drugs, controlling the speed of drug release, and driving the release and can not only adjust the hardness and strength of the preparation but also promote the decomposition [149]. Tang et al. encapsulated PB NPs and aggregation-induced emission luminescence in agarose hydrogels to create a novel injectable nanozyme hydrogel. When hydrogel is under the 808 nm laser irradiation condition, the PB nanozyme in the gel will convert light energy into heat energy, causing hydrogels degradation and releasing active substances into the tumor cells, it further produces large amounts of ROS that mediate cytotoxicity [150]. In terms of biological monitoring, hydrogels have always been a research hotspot due to their good electrical conductivity, especially their controllable extensibility and similar structural characteristics of soft tissues [151]. In addition, the application of hydrogels can be significantly expanded by endowing them with certain adhesion properties, such as wearable biosensors [152]. Lu et al. synthesized a novel nanozyme gel using a polyphenol–metal complexation strategy, in which Ag particles with a size of 2–3 nm were chelated with tannic acid. Due to the dynamic redox balance of phenol–quinone, the gel could have a repeatable adhesion. Moreover, the hydrogels prepared by ultra-small silver NPs can also be catalyzed into different shapes due to their high enzyme-like catalytic activity, which has broad prospects in the medical field [153].

Self-assembly plays an important role in the preparation of nanomaterials by regulating the interaction between particles at the nanometer scale to control the distribution of particles on the whole assembly, so as to spontaneously form ordered structures [154]. Among them, many studies have been published on the use of the interaction between metal ions and amino acids to produce bionic enzymes [155]. For example, in the article published by Song, they used Fe2+, N-(9-fluorenylmethoxycarbonyl)-L-leucine (Fmoc-Leu), and GOx as building blocks to prepare spherical nanozymes with a size of about 161 nm in coordination and non-covalent interaction. Subsequently, the synergistic antibacterial effect was realized through the cascade of the supramolecular GOx and POD [156]. Obviously, nanozymes based on natural biomolecules have amazing potential in future biomedicine.

According to the World Health Organization (WHO), cardiovascular diseases are now the leading cause of death worldwide. In particular, risk factors such as being overweight, obesity, high levels of salt and alcohol have increased. As an indispensable and important substance in human cells, cholesterol is not only involved in a variety of biochemical reactions but also related to many cardiovascular diseases [158,159]. Therefore, in clinical examination, it is very meaningful to use an efficient, fast, and simple way. Tan et al. designed a visible detection method. By taking advantage of the nature that PPy NPs can catalyze the color reaction between H2O2 and TMB, they added PPy nanozyme and cholesterol oxidase (CHOx) into agarose hydrogel and constructed a portable detection kit. The results showed that the method had high selectivity. The detection range and detection limit are 0–100 μM and 3.5 μM, respectively [160]. Mao et al., in the study, found the gourd urea in supramolecular chemistry-after cyclodextrins macrocyclic molecules of a class of new classes with inner POD-like activity [161]. Therefore, based on its unique molecular conformation, assembled to form core-shell with ZIF-8 supermolecular structure, and the encapsulation of CHOx, build a new kind of compound nanostructure of enzyme (Fig. 8 (A)). It is the CB/CHOx@ZIF-8A octahedron structure that makes the catalytic products of central CHOx encounter CB in all directions, greatly improving the efficiency of cascade catalysis. This method allows a detection range of 12.5–500 μM, detection limit calculation is 2.3 μM [162].

Diabetes is a metabolic disease characterized by hyperglycemia, which is one of the common diseases in modern middle-aged and elderly people [168]. The long-term high blood sugar during the period of diabetes will cause dysfunction of various tissues in the body. Therefore, the realization of accurate monitoring of blood glucose is a very basic link. Chen et al. designed a natural nanozyme cascade biosensor by fixing GOx in the internal cavity of HBF-1-C800. In the whole sensing process, different enzyme reactions were used to transform the final color signal. The results showed that there was a linear relationship within 50–800 μM, and the detection line was as low as 31.6 μM [71]. In general, in the cascade glucose detection system, the production of the intermediate H2O2 is the main step to determine the double enzyme reaction [169]. Zhang et al. showed that they prepared a metal-free nanozyme with double enzyme action-carbon nitride (GCN) and obtained the optimal nanozyme activity through improvement-AKCN after adding potassium hydroxide and potassium chloride. Surprisingly, the GOx activity of AKCN catalyzes the production of H2O2 from glucose under visible light induction, while another enzyme activity causes the oxidation of TMB to a dark blue product under dark conditions (Fig. 8(B)). It is used for real-time glucose detection by intelligent microfluidic devices, and the detection limit is 0.8 μM within 30 s [163]. What is important, this work provides a unique insight into charge modification and separation of nanozymes. Aerogel is a kind of 3D porous material with high porosity, large specific surface area, and low density with gas as the dispersed phase [170]. The metal gel has good potential in the field of biosensing by combining the unique chemical characteristics of metal and the 3D structure characteristics of aerogel [171]. Zeng et al. proposed an environmentally friendly glucose detection method and prepared boron and nitrogen double-doped PdRu aerogel (B,N–PdRu) through a reduction reaction (Fig. 8(C)). Due to the rich electron cloud, the catalytic activity is greatly increased and the sensitivity of the detection method is improved. It has a good detection effect on glucose within the range of 10 μM–2 mM, and the detection limit is 6 μM [164]. Similarly, Xu et al. prepared AuPd aerogel by series interaction of different components. The nanozyme had POD-like and GOx enzyme activities. In the colorimetric detection of glucose, the results showed that the detection range of the enzyme was 30–250 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 10 μM [172].

Cancer is a major killer of human life and health, so it is particularly important to realize early prevention and diagnosis to improve the survival rate and cure rate. The naked eye colorimetric method is the most widely used in many analytical sensing methods [173,174]. For example, Wang et al. used Au NPs, which have excellent photothermal properties, to link DNA fragments of programming sequences to Au particles through metal–base interactions to form flower-like nanostructures (Fig. 8(D)). Compared with unmodified gold, AuNFs have more branching structures and a larger surface area ratio, resulting in stronger photothermal effect and enzyme activity, thus greatly improving the sensitivity of the detection of cancer cells, and the colorimetric analysis of human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells can be reduced to 10 cells/mL [165]. In addition, Yang et al. also developed an early cell sensor for electrochemical detection. Circulating tumor cells (CTCs), as a marker mainly present in peripheral blood circulation, are also one of the Food and Drug Administration-approved clinical cell searches [175]. The Apt/Au@PdMo bioconjugates were synthesized by wet chemistry and thio-modification and further immobilized to construct the sensor (Fig. 8(E)). The strong differential pulse voltammetry signal could be generated by using the POD-like activity of the nanozyme and the nano-sensing structure similar to the CTC cell membrane. The linear range of the sensor is 2–1.0 × 105 cells/mL and the detection limit is 2 cells/mL [166]. Furthermore, the level of transferrin receptor (TfR1) expression in malignant proliferating cells was significantly higher than that in normal tissues [176]. Therefore, TfR1 is considered an important target for tumor therapy. Based on the fact that Ftn can bind well with TfR1, Liu et al. constructed a hemin@Ftn nanozyme with POD-like activity and used it as a colorimetric probe to monitor tumor cells by taking advantage of the fact that the hemin@Ftn can catalyze reactions in tumor cells [177]. As a signaling tool, exosomes play a crucial role in mediating tumor development [178]. Therefore, in order to achieve more sensitive detection of tumor exosomes, Li et al. constructed a high-performance photothermal sensor by using CD63 and Mucin-1 double aptamer recognition and significant OXD-like activity of MnO2/IR780. The detection limit is as low as 5.1 × 103 [179]. In addition, the research group further designed an electrochemical sensing platform based on MnO2 nanosheets and verified that it is highly selective for microRNA (let-7a) and has a good linear relationship in the range of 0.4–100 nM, which provides a new idea for expanding the application of nanozymes in tumor prevention and detection [180].

As an important by-product of reactive oxygen metabolism, H2O2 is a key regulator of many oxidative stress-related reactions and is associated with inflammatory reactions such as asthma, atherosclerosis, and osteoporosis. In addition, it is easy to obtain in the body, easily through the cell membrane, so it becomes an important tool to study the oxidative damage of the body [181,182]. NCs, as nanoscale atomic groups, have many applications in biosensing due to their unique microscopic physicochemical properties [183]. Chen et al. combined the advantages of NCs with osmium (Os), which has fixed POD-like activity and modified the nanozymes with bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a protective agent to improve their biocompatibility (Fig. 8(F)). Then, a colorimetric reaction was used to detect H2O2 in different samples, with a linear range of 1.25–200 μM and a low detection limit of 300 nM [167]. In addition, the activity of natural enzymes can be simulated by biomolecular-inspired assembly. For example, Wang et al. used biomimetic strategies to HRP as a model, the Fe (Ⅲ)/Fe (Ⅱ) as the enzyme active center, assembly to human serum albumin @polydopamine skeleton with excellent POD-like activity is formed on nanometer compound, then the catalytic mechanism of studies has shown that the activity of the enzyme was mainly affected by the metal active center. The results show that the designed colorimetric method has high selectivity and sensitivity and the detection range of H2O2 is 0–100 μM and the detection limit is 62 nM [184]. Shan et al. synthesized a metal-organic gel with good POD-like activity using Fe as a template and 1, 10-phenanthroline-2, 9-dicarboxylic acid as ligand. Thanks to the unique network structure of the gel, the abundant Fe-NX active sites in Fe-MOGs are fully exposed and produce a large amount of ROS, which can be further applied to the chemiluminescence reaction of lumino-H2O2 for the detection of H2O2 in cells. The results show that the method has good linearity in the range of 0.01–40 μM, and the detection limit is as low as 2.2 nM [185]. For the detection of physiological indicators, simple operation, low cost, and high sensitivity are the goals pursued by the modern sensing market. Li et al. published a very entertaining study, in which they found that perovskite NPs coated with phospholipid film had good enzyme activity, and unlike other nanozyme catalyzed reactions, did not require additional TMB to spontaneously produce fluorescence quenching of H2O2. With this special property, it provides a general platform for the detection of small molecule metabolites such as lactic acid, uric acid (UA), and cholesterol. Different kinds of phospholipid membrane (PM)–CsPbX3 NCs were modified with different isoelectric points, and diverse targets were marked according to different color reactions. The results showed that the detection limits of lactic acid, UA, and cholesterol were approximately 0.27, 0.87, and 0.41 μM. This study also provides a new approach for later in vitro diagnosis [186].

The colorimetric sensor can be constructed based on various reaction principles. For example, the good catalytic activity of nanomaterials can be used to catalyze the reaction with color change so as to indirectly achieve the analysis and detection of the target [187]. In Chen's study, a Fe55–N–C nanozyme with POD-like activity was prepared. When the antioxidant ascorbic acid (AA) was added to the TMB/H2O2 reaction system containing the nanozyme, the peak value of the solution at 625 nm decreased significantly, and the color of the solution gradually became colorless at the same time [128]. In the process of AA production, alkaline phosphatase plays a certain auxiliary role; it is widely distributed in all tissues and organs of the human body, especially in the liver, which is one of the important indicators for detecting liver diseases [188]. Therefore, the Fe55–N–C nanozyme was used to detect alkaline phosphatase with a detection limit of 0.03 U/L, lower than the results of the optical detection method. Trypsin level, as one of the enzymes in blood biochemical examination, is often used to evaluate the health status of human pancreas. For this reason, some researchers have been exploring to development of simple and economic detection methods [189,190]. Lin's experimental group further modified Pt NPs with citric acid based on the relationship between charge and substrate affinity, and the Cit-Pt NPs also showed stronger OXD-like activity. Subsequently, based on the principle that protamine can be hydrolyzed by trypsin and lose enzyme inhibition, the nanozyme was used to detect the enzyme content in the sample. The detection range of this method was 1.0–70.0 ng/mL, and the detection limit was as low as 0.6 ng/mL [190].

UA is an important metabolic material in the body, the normal human blood UA concentration is about 7 mg/dL, too high or too low represents metabolic problems, such as immune function [191], so the levels of UA in the body often is considered to be one of the measures of gout, arthritis, and other diseases, the content of testing should be simple and accurate. Recent efforts have been spent in exporting the application of nanozyme in UA detecting [192,193]. For example, Hu et al. designed a single-atom catalytic enzyme for the electrochemical detection of UA. With the help of N-doped carbon nanomaterials, which have a series of advantages conducive to catalytic reactions, A-Co-NG was prepared by using Co in transition metal. Excellent experimental results show that the sensor has a wide detection range between 0.4 and 41,950 μM with a detection limit of 33.3 nM [194]. Uracil DNA glycosidase (UDG) is a highly conserved repair protein that prevents gene mismatches or mutations in DNA by removing the mistakenly occurring uracil bases in DNA and maintaining gene integrity [195]. In addition, the level of UDG is also associated with human immunodeficiency [196]. Zhang et al. prepared h-Mn/Ni LDHs with hollow structures by hydrothermal synthesis method. After that, nanozymes were fixed on the electrode surface by hybridization technology, and chemical signals were converted into electrical signals by a catalytic reaction with OXD activity. The detection limit of UDG was 4.8 × 10−4 U/mL [197]. Acetylcholinesterase (AchE) plays an important role in the development of the nervous system and biological signal transduction [198]. In order to increase enzyme activity, cooperative enhancement strategies are often used. For example, in the study reported by Huang, both Co2O4 and ferric tetroxide have the characteristics of POD activity, and the double shell structure Co3O4@Co–Fe oxide nanocages were designed with the help of ZIF-67, while the blue color produced by adding double shell structure Co3O4@Co–Fe oxide nanocages into TMB solution would be inhibited by acetylcholine (ATCh). Therefore, nanozyme is used as a colorimetric biosensor for AchE activity screening, with a linear range of 8.0 × 10−4-1.0 mUmL−1, and the detection limit is 2.0 × 10−4 mUmL−1. Furthermore, it can also be used to screen AchE inhibitors. Among them, huperzine A (HA), a drug for the treatment of Alzheimer’s disease, was detected and the results showed that the semi-inhibitory concentration was 21.2 nM [199].

TME plays a crucial role in tumor growth and metastasis [200]. In order to catch up with the steps of modern precision therapy, more and more researchers are devoted to exploring the detection and sensing of various small molecule substances in TME, so as to carry out the early diagnosis of cancer and monitor the long-term treatment effect. TMB is a safe chromogen compound, which has been widely used in clinical detection. At the same time, the oxidation product can present a strong NIR-Ⅱ photoacoustic signal at 750–1200 nm, thus acting as a specific switch to trigger the redox of H2O2 at the tumor site [201]. However, the water solubility of TMB is very poor. To solve this problem, Ma et al. loaded TMB and ultra-small PtAu nanozyme into the MOF. Meanwhile, based on the good affinity between S and PtAu particles, they added PEG for modification to improve the biocompatibility of the nanozyme. The synthesized TMB/MOF/PtAu-PEG showed better OXD-like and CAT-like activity than normal tissue in TME due to the presence of Au and Pt and significantly improve TMB oxidation color, which was used for tumor site-specific PA imaging (Fig. 9 (A)). At the same time, the ROS generated could also synergically enhance the photothermal ablation of tumor [202].

GSH is a tripeptide formed by the combination of glutamic acid, cysteine, and glycine. As an important regulatory metabolite in cells, GSH is involved in a variety of biochemical reactions in the body [203]. GSH levels in the body have been associated with many diseases, especially at high levels at the site of tumors, and are often used as a marker for tumors [206]. So it is of great practical significance to develop nanozyme for detecting GSH levels. Since GSH can react with H2O2 and oxTMB to form GSH disulfide, causing the solution color to fade from blue to colorless, Wang et al. designed PdFe/GDY with POD-like enzyme activity to detect GSH levels in bacteria at a detection limit of 24.45 nM [86]. Li et al. synthesized Fe3O4@C NPs and also conducted a quantitative analysis of GSH based on the POD activity of nanozyme (Fig. 9(B)). The results showed that the dynamic response range was 20 ng/mL–20 μg/mL, and the detection limit was as low as 200 pg/mL [204]. Ruthenium (Ru) crystals in recent years due to their relatively low price and the characteristics of high catalytic activity widely concerned, given the earlier study found ruthenium NP box has excellent class POD activity [207], Tan et al. use graphene as the carrier, and via chemical vapor deposition to prepare the graphene encapsulation of ruthenium nanocrystalline (Ru@G). They further determine the content of GSH in human serum (Fig. 9(C)), the results show that the detection limit of this method is 0.54 μM and the linear range is 1–60 μM [205]. It has been reported that penicillin can be used as a photochemical reactant of luminol in the presence of HRP. Based on this, Li et al. replaced the poorly water-soluble penicillin with artesunate with good water-solubility and developed BSA-protected Au NCs (BSA@Au NCs)/luminol/artesunate as chemiluminescence (CL) system for GSH detection. GSH, as an inhibitor of ROS catalyzed by nanozyme, blocks CL reaction with luminol. The method works well in the detection range of 50.0–5000.0 nM, with a minimum detection of 5.2 nM [208].

Cancer cells secrete an acidic glycoprotein into body fluids when tumors occur, so carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a broad-spectrum tumor marker [209,210]. Zhu et al. replaced horseradish peroxidase (HRP) with Fe–N–C SAE with excellent POD-like activity for ELISA detection of CEA levels in patient's serum. Compared with commercial ELISA detection results, the detection range of the former reached 1–1000 pg/mL and the limit was reduced from 2 pg/mL to 0.55 pg/mL, indicating that the sensitivity was greatly improved [211], which has important clinical value for the diagnosis and monitoring of malignant tumor in the future. Li et al. prepared Pd–Ir NPs by seed-mediated growth, and CEA was detected by ELISA using POD-like activity. Besides the nanozyme having a certain size effect, the activity of NPs with the same shape but different size as the crystal plane was measured, the results show that in a certain size within the scope of its catalytic activity and particle size are positively correlated and also shows the size dependence in ELISA detection, maximum sensitivity when nanozyme minimum size of 3.3 nm, the detection limit was as low as 3.7 pg/mL, and the detection range was 10–2000 pg/mL [212]. When nanozyme detection is promoted to gradually replace natural enzymes, it is also difficult to maintain the dispersion and stability of ultra-small NPs.

Bacterial infections have been one of the major threats to public health, killing more than 10 million people each year [213]. So far, a variety of methods have been proposed for the detection of bacteria. However, most of the methods, including plate counting, ELISAs [214], polymerase chain reactions (PCR) [215], mass spectrometry [216], flow cytometry [217], and colorimetric biosensors have problems such as time-consuming culture, laborious extraction, or expensive equipment. In contrast, electrochemical detection methods have attracted more and more researchers’ attention because of their advantages of low detection limit, simple operation, and fast signal response [218]. Among various kinds of pathogenic bacteria, especially staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) can quickly adapt to antibiotics, and evolve into antibiotic-resistant bacteria, which can cause many serious infectious diseases [219]. Hu et al. specifically targeted S. aureus with the addition of vancomycin and the second recognition agent (Ab2) in the system and catalyzed the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) in the presence of H2O2 with good POD activity of the Ab2/AuNPs/MOFs. The oxidation product (OPDOX) could be electrochemically detected by the cathodic current of its reduction to OPD. The peak current formed response to the concentration of S. aureus, and the optimal detection limit was 6 colony-forming units (CFU)/mL [97]. Escherichia coil O157: H7 (E. coil O157: H7) is an intestinal and highly contagious food and water-borne pathogen that is resistant to various antibiotics. In order to improve the bacterial targeting ability of Au–Pt nanozyme, Lu et al. vulcanized an E. coil O157: H7 aptamer and coupled it to the nano-dumbbell (Fig. 10 (A)). Magnetic NPs connected with anti-E. coil O157: H7 antibodies were used to capture the target bacteria and sensitively detect E. coil O157: H7, detection limit is 2 CFU/mL, linear range is 10–107 CFU/mL [59]. Though reducing Pt4+ ions to Pt NPs on the surface of carboxyl-functionalized polymer nanospheres (Ps), Hu et al. prepared the nanozyme Ps-Pt with high POD-like catalytic activity. Due to the presence of Pt NPs, the principle of Ps-Pt oxidate TMB and H2O2 to generate blue-colored products and turn yellow after adding H2SO4 can be used to detect the Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhi) from Luria-Bertani broth, spiked milk, and serum samples with the naked eye [220].

The world is now coming together to combat coronavirus disease 2019, a new human infectious disease caused by severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-COV-2) [221]. Interestingly, compared to a reverse transcription-PCR, graphene-based field effect transistor, and immunofluorescence analysis, Fu et al. detected spike proteins with a detection limit of 11 ng/mL, which was lower than several known methods. The process is as follows: the obtained Au@Pt NPs were functionalized by antibodies. The detected antigen is then anchored to specific sites and the remaining unbound sites are blocked using BSA. Finally, antibody-functionalized Au@Pt NPs were introduced to attach the antigen, and TMB oxidation was catalyzed by POD-like activity to realize the detection of the target antigen [138]. Pt@Au NPs with a diameter of about 40 nm was also prepared by using the above two precious metals and further linked with mAb1, mAb1-Pt@Au NPs were obtained. Combined with ELISA, 37 clinical serum samples from hospitals were detected, and the results showed that the new method was accurate and reliable. In addition, compared with the conventional commercial ELISA detection limit, the detection limit of the system is reduced from 100 pg/mL to 10 pg/mL, and the detection time is greatly shortened, which provides a new idea for the popularization and family-oriented antigen detection in the later stage [223]. Yan et al. utilized the ability of Ag NPs to bind to a cysteine on viral spike proteins to design an Ag monoatomic nanozyme supported by TiO2 as a novel antiviral material. Nanozymes not only enhance the adsorption capacity of viruses but also Ag–TiO2 SAN with POD activity will produce a large amount of ROS after being engulfed by macrophages, accelerating the extinction of viruses (Fig. 10(B)). [222]. This also further indicates that nanozymes have certain capabilities in antiviral aspects in the future.

Chirality, as an essential attribute of nature, plays an important physiological role in biological macromolecules and life activities [224]. The vast majority of drugs used in daily life are composed of chiral molecules. Among more than 200 drugs commonly used in clinical practice, there are as many as 114 chiral drugs [225]. For chiral drugs, one isomer is effective while the other may be ineffective or even harmful. Therefore, the identification and detection of drug isomers is a very key point in ensuring drug safety [226].
