The most important drug in the treatment of Alzheimer's disease, levodopa (L-DOPA), can have adverse effects on patients when it changes to dextral. Wang et al. prepared N-doped copper oxide/cobalt oxide nanofibers (N–CuO/CoO NFs) in the ultrasonic environment. Compared with the nanozyme without N, the enzyme activity of the former was significantly increased and the electrochemiluminescence signal was amplified (Fig. 11 (A)). Therefore, the nanofiber can detect L-DOPA sensitively with a detection limit of 0.29 nM [227]. Ren published a study showing that the nanozyme constructed from chiral units is enantioselective for dopamine. Using HRP as a model, Fe and porphyrin units were added to the COF as the active center, and L-histidine (L-His) modification was used to give the chiral nanozyme. As dopamine is one of the chiral substrates of natural HRP, L-His-modified enzyme is highly selective for L-DOPA, and D-DOPA is also selected by D-His@Fe–COF. In addition, adjusting the content and density of His can further optimize the selectivity of nanozyme (Fig. 11(B)) [228].

Acetaminophen (AP), as a common antipyretic and analgesic drug, is widely used in daily life. Some antioxidant substances, such as AA, will be added in effervescent tablets during the content detection of acetaminophen compound preparations, which will interfere with the accuracy of the detection results to a certain extent [229]. Based on other studies [230], He et al. developed a nanozyme for AP detection sensor without AA interference. The CS-Pt NPs modified with chondroitin sulfate not only had ascorbate OXD activity but also had higher OXD-like activity than most Pt-based nanozyme (Fig. 11(C)). The peak data at 450 nm showed a good linear relationship in the range of 0.01–2 mM, the detection limit was 0.01 mM, and the interference generated by AA could be ignored [231].

At present, with the development of the world economy and population growth, food safety has become a major global strategic issue, and more and more governments and consumers around the world attach great importance to it. China's food safety issues are related to pesticide residues, microbial contamination, biological toxins, and other aspects. Therefore, food rapid detection technology is the main means to ensure food safety and more and more attention to the regulatory authorities [232]. Chromatographic and spectroscopic techniques are limited in their large-scale application by instrument and price. Because most the food comes from animals and plants and other natural organisms, their natural ability to identify substances and react, biological detection technology has developed rapidly in recent years [233]. Biotechnology, which uses biological materials to reflect chemical substances in food to achieve the purpose of detection, shows great application potential in food inspection. The widely used methods include ELISA [234], PCR [235], biosensor, and biochip [236].

ELISA is an immunoassay method based on immune enzymology, which combines the high specificity of antigen and antibody reactions with the high catalytic effect of enzymes. In the detection, the enzyme-labeled antigen or antibody is used as the main reagent, and the substance is treated qualitatively or quantitatively by the enzyme-catalyzed substrate color reaction in the complex [237]. In order to achieve high and stable catalytic efficiency, many researchers place natural enzymes with nanozymes with intrinsic enzyme activity [238]. So far, a series of nanozymes, such as MOF, precious metals, and SAE, have been applied to ELISAs. Recently, Li et al. prepared laccase mimics (LM) nanozyme with good laccase-like activity and established a simple and fast ELISA method for the determination of milk allergen protein. During the detection process, the nanozyme modified by the antibody catalyzes the oxidation of 2, 4-DP, and the oxidation product then reacts with the oxidation coupling of 4-AP to produce a red product with an absorption peak at 510 nm. The content of α-LA in raw milk was 1.13 mg/mL. In addition, the author integrates this method with smartphones, which provides some ideas for the detection and intelligent analysis of other substances [239]. Deoxynivalenol (DON) is widely distributed around the world, mainly contaminating wheat, corn, and other grain crops [240]. In recent years, it has been found that DON may be associated with human esophageal cancer, posing a threat to health. Meanwhile, many countries have set strict limit standards for DON content in grain and feed [241]. In addition, some existing commercial ELISA kits on the market are less sensitive. With this situation, Xiong et al. developed AuNPs@Ag loaded with M13 phage (M13DON@AuNPs@Ag) through functional group modification and in situ growth method. The nanozyme was used as enzyme labeled antigen and TMB was used as chromogenic agent. The LOD value obtained by using this new strategy was 13.67 pg/mL. Compared with the traditional method, the sensitivity is more than 100 times higher [242].

Electrochemical biosensors, which use biomolecules (enzymes, antibodies, oligonucleotides, etc.) as recognition elements, are simple to operate, affordable, and multimedical compared with other biosensors, making them reliable tools for determining a wide range of molecular analytes [243]. For the past few years, as nanomaterials have been updated, they even allow in situ measurements to be made directly in flowing complex samples, filling in many of the previous deficiencies [244]. The biomimetic cascade nanoreactor based on nanozyme has high electrochemical signal generation and substrate transformation capacity. Xing et al. designed an artificial peroxisome with multiple enzyme activities—Pd NPs functionalized N-doped porous carbon reduction graphene oxide. Thanks to graphene's excellent electrical conductivity, large surface area, and abundant active sites, and the special sandwich structure accelerates the transport of electrolytes, the designers used this for GSH detection with a concentration range of 70 nm–1500 nm and detection limit of 9.8 nm (S/N = 3) [245].

Compared with the above commonly used several sensors, the colorimetric sensor is based on the change of reaction color, through naked eye observation or with the help of a spectrophotometer and other instruments directly or indirectly qualitative or quantitative detection of the target [246]. Because of its advantages of simple operation, visibility to the naked eye, and low cost, it has been widely used in food and drug detection and other fields [247]. Ali Tuncel et al. obtained monodisperse porous CeO2 microspheres and used the absorbability of porous microspheres to separate phosphoprotein from milk. Moreover, the concentration of phosphoprotein adsorbed was negatively correlated with the POD-like enzyme activity of CeO2. Therefore, the determination of phosphoprotein concentration in milk by using microspheres as nanozyme sensors is up to 400 μg/mL [248]. However, during the detection process, try to be careful about the interference of the color of the sample itself on the color development results.

H2O2 is used as a food additive in many countries because of its good oxidability, for example, to inhibit microbial growth in milk [249]. However, excessive H2O2 can cause oxidative stress in the body, so it is necessary to monitor the content of H2O2 in food. SERS has gained more attention in the sensing field in recent years due to its unique characteristics. It can obtain much more signals than ordinary Raman scattering and can be used for the configuration analysis of biological macromolecules and provide interface information [250]. It is suitable for the determination of some biological minimum concentrations [251]. Siddiqui et al. obtained Mn3O4–Au nanocomposites with spindle structure by hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. 12 (A)). It was used as a SERS probe to detect H2O2 content in milk, and the detection limit of the sensor was 0.6 μM [252]. Food allergy has always been a great challenge to human food health, among which milk allergy is considered one of the most common food allergic reactions [253]. Zhang et al. designed a method for the detection of α-LA, one of the allergens in milk, by modification of anti-α-LA polyclonal antibody on the surface of CeO2 nanozyme with POD-like activity. The obtained CeO2@Ab2 was used as the “on” of the Raman signal in the catalytic reaction, and the LOD of α-LA by this nanozyme-enhanced SERS immunoassay method was 0.01 ng/mL (Fig. 12(B)) [254].

Aflatoxin (AF) is classified as a class of natural carcinogens by the WHO, among which derivative B1 is the most toxic. Mainly contaminated grain, oil, and its products, as well as all kinds of plant and animal food can also be contaminated [255]. In view of this, the governments of various countries have formulated the maximum allowable amount of AF in food and feed respectively. So, rapid and accurate detection of AFB1 in food is momentous for food safety at this stage [256,257]. Shi et al. further improved the fluorescence detection method by combining it with photothermal and colorimetric signals. Under acidic conditions, PB particles were grown in situ on the negative carrier of magnetic NPs to obtain MNPs@PBNPs. With the help of the photothermal effect and POD-like activity, the colorimetric signal obtained under the irradiation of NIR was significantly amplified, and the detection limit of AFB1 was 0.54 fg/mL [258].

Microfluidic chip technology, as a product of multidisciplinary cross set, is mainly studied in integration, especially in biosensor [259,260]. For example, Lin's laboratory developed a novel sensor for the detection of food-derived Salmonella. The sensor consists of three parts. First, Fe-MOF/PtNPs nanozyme prepared by hydrothermal synthesis method is used to improve the catalytic efficiency of MOF with a large specific surface area as a signal amplifier. A mixer is further used to combine the target bacteria and nanozyme in the microfluidic chip. H2O2 is added to the sandwich, and the generated O2 is used to promote the combination of water and CaO to produce heat. Finally, the heat is measured based on the thermal sensor, and the number of Salmonella is calculated according to a series of linear relationships (Fig. 12(C)). The detection limit of this method is 93 CFU/mL, which also provides a new idea for the portable detection of other pathogens [261].

As we know, organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) kill insects by inhibiting AchE activity to ensure the growth of crops. However, OPs residues can be detected in most foods and accumulate through the food chain, eventually posing a threat to human health [262]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a variety of sensitive selective OPs sensors. Based on the unique chemical structure and advantages of MXene, Li et al. synthesized the flake cobalt-doped Ti3C2 MXene nanozyme by wet chemical method and developed an electrochemical sensor for pesticide residue detection combined with its excellent POD-like activity and high responsiveness to thiol compounds [263]. Chen et al. prepared Au@Pt from AuNPs as seed. In the detection process, first, AuNP probes and Au@Pt probes react through complementary pairing. OPs then compete with MNP probes to bind monoclonal antibodies to AuNP probes. Finally, the MNP and AuNP probes were separated using magnetic fields, and the released Au@Pt probe reacted with carboxyl-modified mercapto oligonucleotides on an amino-modified black plate to catalyze the production of fluorescein from the non-fluorescent substrate of AmplexTMRed (AR) for OPs use [264]. In the study published by Xu, they selected Mn2O3 NPs with the strongest OXD activity. After the protein adsorption experiment results were not satisfactory, they chose the coupling method. Glutaraldehyde was used as the crosslinking agent to connect the antibody to the surface of the nanozyme, and the content of OPs in the sample was determined with this method. The detection limit of one-step icELISA was 20.4 ng/mL, and had an excellent linear relationship in the concentration range of 52.3–1309.6 ng/mL [265]. To simplify the detection procedure and the use of valuable instruments, Li et al. based on glyphosate can inhibit the catalytic reaction of POD, combining V2O5 nanosheets with paper-based analysis devices, and the color change during the reaction is visible to the naked eye [266]. For highly toxic OPs, it is very critical to hydrolyze them into low-toxicity molecules through a dephosphorylation reaction to reduce pollution to the surrounding environment, so the team further designed N-doped carbon groups as the substrate of nanozymes, in which Ce(IV)/Ce(III) is the active site, which exerts outstanding phosphatase-like activity [267].

Phenolic compounds are the basic raw materials of the organic chemical industry. Because most of them have high solubility in water, they become one of the main pollutants in water. When the concentration is higher than 5 mg/mL, it can cause fish poisoning and death [268]. Laccase is known as an “environmental protection enzyme”. In recent years, with the enhancement of environmental awareness, laccase has attracted more and more attention from scholars [269]. Such as Kim et al. prepared a kind of laccase nanoflower and self-assembled Cu2+ and DNA fragments. The stability of the enzyme was enhanced by the flower-like support structure (Fig. 13 (A)). Then, a simple paper microfluidic device was designed, and the linear range of several phenolic compounds was up to 25 μg/mL. The detection limit of hydroquinone (HQ) was 4.5 μg/mL [270]. In addition to using active metal (Cu) as raw material, Lee prepared coral-like nanozyme with laccase activity by using silver nitrate and sodium citrate of a certain mole ratio. AgCit can also quantitatively determine and degradation of various phenolic pollutants (Fig. 13(B)) [271]. HQ, as a raw material for the preparation of pesticides, has strong toxicity and is listed as one of the main environmental pollutants by the European Union [272]. Therefore, to achieve more accurate and efficient detection, Zhan et al. developed porous Co3O4 nanosheets as a colorimetric sensor of HQ. Due to the use of the sodium borohydride reduction method in the synthesis process, in addition to the bubbles produced increased the nanopore structure, but formed many Lewis acid sites on the surface, which induced the aggregation and absorption of electron-rich substrates in the catalytic process, and the enzyme-like activity was significantly higher than that of ordinary Co3O4. Therefore, the detection results of lake water samples show that the linear range was 5.0–1000 μM, and the detection limit is as low as 0.58 μM [273]. More intriguingly, Zou et al. designed a colorimetric sensor for detecting heavy metal content in water based on the excellent POD-like activity of Co3O4 [274]. As it is well known, water is the source of human life, and its quality should be strictly controlled. Compared with the traditional method of heavy metal detection, the colorimetric method is more and more popular. In this study, the Co3O4 nanozyme was synthesized by the soft template method, and the characterization results showed that the enzyme was in the shape of a porous disk. Due to the inhibition of some heavy metals on enzyme activity, the nanozyme can detect Cd (ⅱ), Hg (ⅱ), Pb (ⅱ), and As (ⅱ) in samples with a detection limit as low as 0.085 μg/L, and has good repeatability, the average recovery of heavy metals after washing is 86.9%–98.3%.

As a radioactive element, uranium is the fuel of clean energy-nuclear energy, but when nuclear accidents or nuclear leakage, it will bring many problems to the ecological environment, especially the WHO rules that the maximum content of UO2 2+ in drinking water is 30 μg/L [275]. Zhang et al. designed a nanozyme sensor based on the colorimetric method and made use of the advantages of high stability and adjustable structure of COFs by co-combining Tph and 5,5-difluoro-2,8-diformyl-1,3,7,9-tetramethyl-10-phenyl-5H-dipyrrolo[1,2-c:2',1'-f][1,3,2]diazabori-nin-4-ium-5-uide (BDP) to produce an organic covalent framework. When TMB and TPH-BDP exist simultaneously, the solution color changes visibly by adding UO2 2+. Further sample inspection shows good linearity within 0.18–75 μM, with a LOD of 0.05 μM [276].

In this review, we summarized the research progress of different dimensions of nanozyme to discuss the influence of structure on enzyme activity. Furthermore, the nanozyme-based various types of biosensors to biological analysis and diagnosis were also commented.

A variety of factors such as the shape, size, and surface modification of nanozymes can directly affect the activity, and nanozymes in different dimensions cannot be normalized and compared in activity, but each has its characteristics. As the smallest class, 0D nanozymes not only have a significant surface volume ratio but also have unique optical properties that bring interesting applications for biosensing, such as fluorescent probes and photothermal sensors [277]. In contrast, 1D and 2D nanozymes have impressive electron transport speeds and electrical signal transduction efficiencies and are widely used in the design of electrochemical sensors. The development of highly active nanozymes based on 3D MOF and other structures has become a hot spot, including the design of biomimetic cascade nanoreactors, and the exploration of enzyme catalytic mechanisms has made significant progress. However, there are still problems and challenges in the design and preparation of high-efficiency nanozymes.

To meet the needs of practical applications, the catalytic performance can be regulated by adjusting the composition, shape, size, and so on of nanozymes. Therefore, the synthesis of nanozymes is an opening door to the construction of highly active nanozymes. Even if researchers use physical or chemical methods to synthesize a series of different nanozymes, there are still technical difficulties that need to be improved, such as small size 0D NPs are easily oxidized and agglomerated in the air, and their performance is unstable, and 1D nanozymes such as nanotubes often used to design electrochemical sensors and 2D nanosheets represented by graphene have problems such as difficulty in achieving fine structural control, high preparation cost, and long time. In addition, to meet the development of modern green chemistry and promote the industrialization of nanozymes, new synthetic methods need to be explored by researchers, such as biosynthesis based on various types of microorganisms [278].

In general, no matter what dimension of nanozyme, its structure–activity relationship has always been the core of the future expansion of nanozyme fields. Changes in physical and chemical properties such as shape, size, and surface structure may bring unexpected performance. For the discovered natural nanozymes, the allosteric effect of its fourth-order structure enables the enzyme to continuously adjust its activity in vivo, better adapt to environmental changes, and complete physiological functions more effectively. In contrast, in the design of nanozymes, researchers can reverse select morphology and structure from the desired function depending on the application [279].

For the application of nanozymes in biosensing, high sensitivity and selectivity have always been the focus of researchers' development process [280]. Modifying some corresponding aptamers or antibodies on the surface of nanozymes can often increase the selectivity of enzymes, but it is necessary to pay attention to the influence of some modification designs on enzyme activities. In addition, the specificity and singleness of enzyme activities are also important factors affecting sensing, and other unrelated activities are excluded from interfering with the reaction.

For existing cascade biomimetic nanozymes, most use GOx as the first reaction enzyme for detecting glucose. Therefore, novel nanozyme cascade reactors designed to detect other analytes are an important part of future developments. Among them, the use of molecular imprinting technology and computer simulation to screen and construct suitable fixed enzyme frameworks is a very effective means. Researchers can fully combine the characteristics of different dimensions in the design process, especially 3D nanomaterials, and use carrier scaffolds to achieve precise control of the spatial structure of guest enzymes. In addition, achieving the recognition of multiple objects simultaneously for high-throughput detection can also significantly improve the utility of nanozymes in sensing.

With the continuous improvement of medical standards and the quality of health services, health monitoring has become an increasing focus of attention. Monitoring biomarker levels to analyze health is a common method, but most nanozymes are used to build in vitro sensing platforms, like today's very hot wearable sensors based on 1D and 2D nanomaterials, which can detect daily blood glucose concentrations. Very few nanozymes can actually enter the body to exert enzyme activity and achieve sensing analysis at the cellular level [281]. The complex and changeable in vivo environment not only requires nanozymes to be highly selective but also metabolic and biological toxicity in vivo has always been a problem that has plagued this development. Therefore, the screening of synthetic materials and the preparation of 0D ultra-small size nanozymes can become the focus of breakthroughs.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mengying A received her B.Sc. in 2021 from School of Veterinary Medicine, Sichuan Agricultural University. Currently, she is a postgraduate student in institute of translational medicine, Nanchang University. Her research interests focus on nanozymes and biomedicines.

Yongjian Ai received his Ph.D in 2021 from Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University under supervise of Professor Qionglin Liang. Then, he worked an associate professor at Nanchang University. Currently he is a research associate in Tsinghua University. His research interests focus on biofabrication, bioanalysis and biomedicines, published over 60 peer-reviewed papers.

Hongbo Xin received his Ph.D in 1991 from Department of Pharmacology, Beijing University Medical Center. He moved to Vanderbilt University as a postdoctoral fellow from 1993. In 1999, he worked as a research assistant professor at the University of Pennsylvania and appointed as an assistant professor at Cornell University in 2000. In 2006, he became the professor at Sichuan University. Since 2009, he became the director of institute of translational medicine, Nanchang University. His researches focus on cardiovascular diseases, bioengineering drugs and stem cells, published over 150 peer-reviewed papers.

Qionglin Liang received both his Ph.D (2005) and B.Sc. (2000) from Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University. Currently he is a chair professor in Department of Chemistry at Tsinghua University. His research interests focus on the development of bio-inspired microfluidics techniques and their applications in biomaterials, biofabrication and organ-on-a-chip, published over 200 peer-reviewed papers.
